questions.prompt: What does Selenium actually search when it looks for an element?
options: The raw HTML text of the page
The browser's live DOM tree of element nodes
questions.explanation: The browser parses HTML into the DOM — a live tree of nodes — and Selenium locates and acts on those nodes, not the raw HTML.

questions.prompt: Why is a well-chosen id usually the most reliable locator?
options: ids are always shorter than other locators
An id is meant to be unique and tends to stay stable as layout and styling change
Only ids can be used with Selenium
ids automatically update when the page changes
questions.explanation: A good id uniquely identifies one element and rarely changes with restyling/relayout, so the locator keeps matching.

questions.prompt: Which locator is the MOST brittle?
questions.explanation: A deep, position-based absolute XPath breaks the moment the surrounding layout shifts, even though the target element still exists.

questions.prompt: A locator matches three elements on the page. What is the problem?
options: Nothing — Selenium picks the best one
A locator should match exactly one element; matching several makes actions ambiguous and tests unreliable
It means the page has a bug
Selenium will always act on all three
questions.explanation: A good locator is unique. Matching multiple elements makes 'find the element' ambiguous — you may act on the wrong one, causing flaky tests.
